subject: Internal medicine
title: Traumatic hemothorax
preambleMarkdown:
# Traumatic hemothorax

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Traumatic hemothorax is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Traumatic hemothorax to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Traumatic hemothorax is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Traumatic hemothorax depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Traumatic hemothorax.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Traumatic hemothorax disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Traumatic hemothorax should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Traumatic hemothorax first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Traumatic hemothorax, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Traumatic hemothorax, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Traumatic hemothorax presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Traumatic hemothorax with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Traumatic hemothorax is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Traumatic hemothorax should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Traumatic hemothorax is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Traumatic hemothorax, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Traumatic hemothorax may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Traumatic hemothorax patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Traumatic hemothorax, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Traumatic hemothorax in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Traumatic hemothorax, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Traumatic hemothorax does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Traumatic hemothorax may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Traumatic hemothorax, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Traumatic hemothorax as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Traumatic hemothorax draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Thoracic Trauma

_Source lines: 33132-33338._

```text
VII. THORACIC TRAUMA
A. Discussion. Chest injuries account for 25% of trauma deaths. Approximately 15%
of traumatic chest injuries require operative intervention; the remaining 85% can
be managed in the emergency department. The cardiac (anterior) box is the most
worrisome region. The boundaries are the sternomanubrial angle (superiorly),
lower ribs (inferiorly), and the right and left nipples (lateral).

B. Clinical features
1. Simple pneumothorax is a collection of air in the pleural space that does not
communicate with the external chest wall. Findings include decreased breath
sounds and hyperresonance on the affected side, dyspnea, and chest pain.

Chest wall injury may or may not be present.

2. Open pneumothorax. "Sucking chest wound" is a defect in the chest wall. If
the wound is greater than two-thirds of the diameter of the trachea, air may
enter the chest through the wound rather than through the normal airway,
causing collapse of the ipsilateral lung and ineffective respiration.

3. Tension pneumothorax is a continuous accumulation of air in pleural space
with no available exit. This increased intrapleural pressure leads to mediasti-
nal shift and compression of the contralateral lung and great vessels, resulting
in deceased cardiac filling, decreased cardiac output, and severe respiratory
compromise. Findings include dyspnea, agitation, cyanosis, tachypnea, sub-
cutaneous emphysema, hypotension, tachycardia, increased jugular venous
pressure, decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance, tracheal shift to the unin-
volved side, and increased resistance to ventilation.

4. Hemothorax occurs when there is blood found in the chest cavity. It may be sec-
ondary to lung injury or bleeding from intercostal or internal mammary vessels.

Massive hemothorax is defined as blood loss of greater than 1,500 mL into the
chest cavity. Decreased breath sounds or dullness may be found on the affected side.

5. Flail chest is when two or more ribs are broken in two or more places. There
is a paradoxic inward motion of the flail segment with inspiration and out-
ward movement on expiration. Crepitus and abnormal chest wall motion may
be present. This paradoxical chest wall motion, underlying pulmonary contu-
sion, and significant pain lead to hypoxia and hypercapnia.

Quick 	HIT
Zone II injuries require surgi-
cal exploration.

Quick 	HIT
The presence of a "machinery
murmur" indicates a possible
air embolus.

Quick 	HIT
Penetrating injuries within
the cardiac box are at risk of
involving the heart and great
vessels.

Quick 	HIT
Tension pneumothorax is
a clinical diagnosis that
requires immediate decom-
pression (you should not
wait for a confirmatory chest
X-ray).

Quick 	HIT
Blunting of the costophrenic
angles generally is not seen
on an upright chest radio-
graph until approximately
250 mL of blood has been
lost.

406 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Traumatic Emergencies
6. Pulmonary contusion is a parenchymal lung injury that results in interstitial
edema and capillary damage. Patients present with dyspnea, tachypnea, tachy-
cardia, hypoxia, and chest pain. Opacifications may be seen immediately on
chest radiograph and always within 6 hours. If opacifications persist on the
chest radiograph for longer than 48 hours, a pulmonary laceration should
be suspected.

7. Tracheobronchial injuries result in labored or noisy breathing,
subcutaneous emphysema, and hemoptysis. Most blunt injuries to the
bronchus occur within 2.5 cm of the carina and are due to a deceleration
mechanism. Persistence of a large air leak or pneumothorax after placement
of a chest tube for pneumothorax should raise the suspicion for a major
bronchial tear.

8. Cardiac contusion should be suspected if a patient has sustained blunt
trauma to the chest. The patient may complain of chest wall or retrosternal
pain. Various atrial and ventricular dysrhythmias, conduction disturbances,
altered cardiac wall motion, and decreased cardiac output may be present.

The electrocardiogram may be normal, and the most commonly encountered
rhythm is sinus tachycardia. Permanent sequelae are uncommon after cardiac
contusion.

9. Pericardial tamponade may occur after blunt or penetrating trauma to the
chest. Bleeding into the nondistensible pericardial sac increases intrapericar-
dial volume and pressure, leading to decreased cardiac output. Beck triad may
be seen and consists of increased central venous pressure (jugular venous
distention), decreased BP, and muffled heart tones. Electrical alternans
(alternating morphology and amplitude of the QRS complex) and diffuse
low voltage (short QRS complexes) may be found on the electrocardiogram.

Removal of even small amounts of fluid (20 to 30 mL) can lead to significant
improvement in patients with acute pericardial tamponade.

10. Traumatic rupture of the aorta may be caused by bending, shearing, or tor-
sional forces on the aorta (see Clinical Pearl 17-4). The most common area of
injury of the aorta is just distal to the ligamentum arteriosum. The descend-
ing aorta is relatively fixed by the ligamentum arteriosum and the intercostal
arteries as compared to the relatively mobile ascending aorta. An intact adven-
titial layer may contain the hematoma and allow the patient to survive until
arrival at the hospital.

a. Causes. Traumatic rupture of the aorta is caused by deceleration of greater
than 30 mph, a fall from greater than 30 feet, or sudden compression of
the chest (car falling on chest).

b. Symptoms. The patient may have minimal symptoms and no apparent
chest wall injury. A minority of patients will have retrosternal or intrascap-
ular pain, dyspnea, stridor, or dysphagia.

11. Diaphragmatic injury may follow either blunt or penetrating trauma. It
must be ruled out in all patients with thoracoabdominal penetrating injuries.

Quick 	HIT
Persistent pneumothorax
after chest tube insertion
may indicate a tracheobron-
chial injury.

Quick 	HIT
Beck triad consists of jugular
venous distention, hypo-
tension, and muffled heart
tones.

Quick 	HIT
A widened mediastinum is
the most common radio-
graphic abnormality seen on
chest X-ray with a traumatic
aortic rupture.

Radiographic Findings of Traumatic Rupture of the Aorta
1. Widened mediastinum
2. Obscured aortic knob
3. Obliteration of the aortopulmonary window (the space between the pulmonary artery and the aorta)
4. Presence of an apical pleural cap
5. Deviation of the trachea or esophagus
6. Fracture of the first or second rib
7. Elevation and rightward displacement of the right mainstem bronchus
8. Depression of the left mainstem bronchus
9. Widening of the paravertebral stripe
PEARL	CLINICAL 	17-4
T R A U M A T I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	407

Traumatic Emergencies
The patient may present with respiratory distress, and a chest radiograph may
show viscera or a nasogastric tube in the chest.

12. Rib fractures mandate a search for other injuries and are primarily of concern
because of the structures that the ribs cover. Pain with breathing may impair
ventilation secondary to splinting.

C. Diagnostic imaging studies
1. Blunt chest trauma patients should have a chest X-ray followed by a chest CT
angiogram if indicated.

2. Penetrating chest injury. All patients with penetrating injury to the chest
should have a chest radiograph performed. If negative, an expiratory film or
CT of the chest can be done. A pneumothorax may be more evident with the
lung in expiration. If all films are negative, the patient should have a CT of the
chest or repeat chest radiograph in 4 to 6 hours.

D. Treatment
1. Pneumothorax and hemopneumothorax are treated with a tube thoracostomy.

a. Chest tube placement is at the third or fourth intercostal space, anterior
axillary line on the affected side. The tube size does not matter in treating
hemothorax versus pneumothorax.

b. Indications for open thoracotomy
i. Initial blood loss through the chest tube of 1,500 mL
ii. Ongoing blood loss of approximately 300 mL/hour
iii. Persistent hypotension after adequate blood replacement
iv. Radiographic evidence of increased hemothorax despite functioning
chest tubes
v. Worsening of the patient's clinical condition
2. Tension pneumothorax may be treated initially with insertion of a 14-gauge
angiocatheter at the second intercostal space in the midclavicular line to relieve
the tension followed quickly by a chest tube.

3. Suspected pericardial tamponade. Pericardiocentesis may be performed while
awaiting formal thoracotomy.

E. Disposition. Patients with stab wounds who are responsible and who have no evi-
dence of intrathoracic penetration on a repeat chest X-ray after 4 to 6 hours may
be discharged home. Most patients with thoracic trauma require admission to a
monitored bed, ICU, or operating suite for further care.
```

### Source Excerpt: Thoracic Trauma

_Source lines: 2473-2654._

```text
Ill. Thoracic Trauma
A. Chest wall trauma and pulmonary contusion

## 1 . Sternal, multiple rib, and scapular fractures often result from a large force and

can be indicative of underlying cardiac or pulmonary contusions .

T R A U M A AN D B U R N S e 33
2. Pain from rib fractures can impair oxygenation and ventilation, and manage
ment consists in providing adequate analgesia and pulmonary care.

a. Mild rib fractures may be managed with oral analgesics , whereas more
severe ones may require an intravenous route or even an epidural.

3. Flail chest occurs when more than two ribs are fractured in two or more
places.

a. Paradoxical movement of the chest when breathing
b. Primary problem is severe pulmonary contusion
4. Pulmonary contusion can impair gas exchange and cause respiratory failure.

a. Managed with supportive care and avoiding fluid overload
b. Sometimes requires mechanical ventilation
B. Hemothorax
l . Can be from blunt or penetrating trauma
a. Intercostal arterial injury can result from a blunt force to the ribs.

b . Massive hemothorax suggests possibility of injury to great vessels , hilum,
or heart.

## 2. Treatment

a. Chest tube in fifth intercostal space
b. Chest tube output is an indication for emergency operative intervention.

( l ) Greater than 1 ,500 mL immediately when chest tube placed
(2) Evidence of ongoing bleeding
(3) Greater than 200 mUhr over 4 hours
C. Pneumothorax
l . Simple pneumothorax
a. Diagnosed on chest X-ray (CXR) ; sometimes see a deep sulcus sign
b. Can convert to tension pneumothorax if untreated
c. Treatment: chest tube placed in fifth intercostal space
2. Open pneumothorax is a sucking chest wound.

a. Large defect in chest wall causing equilibration between intrathoracic and
atmospheric pressure
b. Cover defect and tape on three sides to allow for air to escape on
fourth side.

c. Place chest tube .

D . Tracheobronchial tree injuries are injuries t o trachea or bronchus.

l. Blunt trauma injury usually occurs within l inch of the carina.

## 2 . Signs and symptoms : hemoptysis; tension pneumothorax; pneumothorax with

massive air leak; CXR with completely collapsed, nonventilated lung
3. Diagnosis is made with bronchoscopy.

4. Treatment is to intubate mainstem bronchus of opposite lung.

a. Place chest tube on affected side.

b. Immediate surgical intervention
E. Cardiac tamponade
l . Pericardium fills with blood, preventing cardiac filling and constricting the heart,
causing hemodynamic collapse. Penetrating trauma is a more common cause.

## 2 . Signs

a. Beck triad: elevated central venous pressure, decreased blood pressure, and
mueled heart tones
b. Kussmaul sign: rise in venous pressure with inspiration when breathing
spontaneously
3 . Transthoracic ultrasound has high sensitivity.

## 4. Treatment

a. Fluid bolus to help venous return and cardiac output
b. Temporizing maneuver is with pericardiocentesis .

c. Operative repair is with thoracotomy or median sternotomy, depending on
injury.

 Blunt cardiac injury
l . Etiology: can result from myocardial muscle contusion, valve disruption, and
chamber rupture

S i g n s of p o s s i b l e tra u m atic
a o rtic injury o n CXR: wide
m e d i asti n u m , o b literation
of a o rtic k n o b , deviati o n of
tra c h e a to the rig ht, o b l it
eration of a o rto p u l m o n a ry
w i n d ow. d e p ression of l eft
m a instem b r o n c h u s, d e v i a
tion of e s o p h a g u s to rig ht,
wide p a ratra c h e a l stripe,
and p l e u r a l cap.
2. Signs and symptoms
a. Most common presentation is arrhythmias.

b. Pump failure, leading to low cardiac output, respiratory distress

## 3. Diagnosis

a. Twelve-lead electrocardiogram: If any new abnormalities, patient should be
monitored for 24 hours.

b . Common dysrhythmias include premature ventricular contractions and
right bundle branch block, and may progress to ventricular tachycardia.

c. Patient may show ischemic changes.

d. An echocardiogram may show wall dysfunction.

4. Treatment is supportive . Inotropes, diuretics, antiarrhythmics, and a balloon
pump may be needed.

G. Traumatic aortic injury
l . This is a common cause of sudden death after deceleration blunt injury. There
is a transection of the aorta at the ligamentum arteriosum (distal to the left
subclavian artery) .

2. Diagnosis and management
a. CXR: wide mediastinum (Fig. 3-2)
b . Arteriography or helical contrast-enhanced CT
c. Some require endovascular or open repair.

H. Esophageal injuries
l . Signs and symptoms
a. An early sign or symptom is a left pneumothorax without a rib fracture,
particulate matter in chest tube , or mediastinal air.

b. May present late as septic shock
2. Diagnosis (both of these are required to complete the workup)
a. Esophagoscopy
b. GastrograHn swallow

## 3. Treatment

a. Broad-spectrum antibiotics
b. Early treatment includes primary repair and drainage .

c. Late treatment includes wide pleural drainage and cervical esophagostomy.

esophageal deviation, and b lurring of the aortic knob.
```

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
